name: led
description: Basic LED controlled via GPIO pin. Simplest output device — good for testing GPIO and as a status indicator.

wiring.resistor: Between GPIO pin and LED anode

esp32_constraints.usable_pins: Any GPIO except 6-11 (flash). 34-39 are input-only (OK here since we're outputting).
esp32_constraints.max_current: 12mA per GPIO pin. With 220Ω at 3.3V: ~6mA — safe.
esp32_constraints.pwm_pins: All output-capable GPIOs support PWM (for brightness control).

digital.code:
from machine import Pin
import time

led = Pin(2, Pin.OUT)  # GPIO 2 = built-in LED on many ESP32 boards

# Blink 3 times
for _ in range(3):
    led.on()            # or led.value(1)
    time.sleep(0.5)
    led.off()           # or led.value(0)
    time.sleep(0.5)

led.off()  # ensure off when done

pwm.code:
from machine import Pin, PWM
import time

led = PWM(Pin(2), freq=1000, duty=0)  # 1kHz, off

# Fade in
for duty in range(0, 1024, 16):
    led.duty(duty)
    time.sleep_ms(20)

# Fade out
for duty in range(1023, -1, -16):
    led.duty(duty)
    time.sleep_ms(20)

led.duty(0)  # off

builtin.code:
import machine
import time

led = machine.Pin(2, machine.Pin.OUT)  # GPIO 2 is built-in on ESP32-DevKitC
for _ in range(5):
    led.value(not led.value())
    time.sleep(0.3)
builtin.note: GPIO 2 is the built-in LED on most ESP32 dev boards. Some boards use GPIO 5, 13, or 22 instead.

pitfalls: FORGETTING the current-limiting resistor will burn out the LED and possibly the GPIO pin.
GPIO 6-11 are connected to internal SPI flash — DO NOT USE for LEDs.
Pin(2, Pin.OUT) on ESP32-DevKitC controls the built-in LED — may conflict with other uses.
PWM at very low duty cycles (< 10) may produce visible flicker on some LEDs.
